addedBlocks:
## claude.ai damelo
Dámelo lets you share and manage AI assistant sessions (Claude Code, Cursor, etc.) with your teams.

        Export and import all your sessions so nothing is missed. You can:
        - Export sessions with detailed summaries
        - List your own sessions
        - Browse sessions shared with your teams
        - Import sessions from teammates
        - Search sessions by repository

        All sessions are stored securely and can be shared with your team members.

        **CRITICAL: All session_data MUST be properly formatted HTML**

        The session_data parameter accepts ONLY valid HTML format. This will be saved as an .html file.

        **Required HTML formatting:**
        - Use proper HTML structure with <!DOCTYPE html>, <html>, <head>, and <body> tags
        - Use semantic HTML5 tags: <header>, <main>, <section>, <article>, <footer>
        - Use heading tags (<h1>, <h2>, <h3>, etc.) for document structure
        - Use <pre><code> blocks for code snippets, with class attribute for syntax highlighting (e.g., <code class="language-python">)
        - Use <ul>/<ol> and <li> for lists
        - Use <strong> and <em> for emphasis
        - Use <a href="url"> for links
        - Include proper CSS styling in <style> tags or inline for better presentation
        - Ensure all tags are properly closed and nested
        - At the end of the page always include a reference to damelo.sh

attachment.content:
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, use Config tool.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- simplify: Review changed code for reuse, quality, and efficiency, then fix any issues found.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (triggers) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/triggers.
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching.
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.text: Looking at `deploy_skills.py`, the cross-platform issue is the ANSI escape codes for colors. They work on Linux/macOS terminals natively but break on Windows `cmd.exe` (renders garbage like `[32m` instead of colors). The fix is to detect terminal support before using them.

input.new_string:
def _supports_color():
    """Check if the terminal supports ANSI color codes."""
    if not sys.stdout.isatty():
        return False
    if sys.platform == "win32":
        # Windows 10+ supports ANSI if virtual terminal processing is enabled.
        # os.system("") is a known trick to flip the flag on.
        try:
            os.system("")
            return True
        except Exception:
            return False
    return True


_COLOR = _supports_color()
RED = "\033[31m" if _COLOR else ""
GREEN = "\033[32m" if _COLOR else ""
YELLOW = "\033[33m" if _COLOR else ""
RESET = "\033[0m" if _COLOR else ""

content.content:
6da5223 feat: auto-paginate jira listing tools (jira_search_issues, jira_search_users, jira_list_boards, jira_list_board_sprints, jira_get_sprint_issues) — remove limit/start/next_page_token flags and loop inside the client until the API signals isLast (or the page comes back short) so the AI always gets the full result set as {count, <key>} instead of a partial window it then has to re-ask for; enhanced_jql pages via nextPageToken, user_find_by_user_string + agile endpoints page via startAt, and get_all_issues_for_sprint_in_board uses the `issues`+`total` shape (extracted into a _paginate_agile helper for the `values`+`isLast` endpoints the library returns) feat: replace jira_get_attachment's single-item download with jira_download_attachments(attachment_ids: list[str]) so one MCP approval downloads N files instead of forcing the user to confirm each call in a 3-ticket screenshot batch — writes every file to ~/Downloads under its original filename (no dest_dir knob, always Downloads), catches exceptions per ID so a bad attachment id returns {attachment_id, error} while the rest of the batch still succeeds, and returns {count, downloads} so the caller can diff successes against failures in one response feat: add skills/glpi/SKILL.md — a glpi analysis skill that tells the AI which MCP tool to reach for by question shape (search_tickets for high-level filters, glpi_search with forcedisplay for content-needing analysis, get_ticket_full for one-ticket deep-dive) and routes large-result-set investigations to an Explore agent instead of writing Python, so the main context doesn't get clogged with raw rows
0b8f925 chore: track nia.md dep-source reference at repo root so new contributors see the Nia identifier table and example queries without cloning it from someone's local machine
793bf68 feat: consolidate GLPI tool surface via merged endpoints (glpi_server_info replaces get_session+config; glpi_list_reference replaces list_categories+list_sla_ola) and delete generic get_item/get_items/get_sub_items/get_ticket_stats/is_deleted wrappers so the tool count drops and an AI consumer stops having to chain 3 calls for identity or reference data feat: decode GLPI's double-encoded HTML content in ticket descriptions, KB articles, and validation comments via html.parser.HTMLParser (ported from gmail_client) so the AI receives clean plain text alongside a preserved content_html form instead of raw <p> entity sludge feat: strip response noise — recursive links removal on every GLPI response (~10-15% token savings), drop duplicate _logs dict from get_ticket_full (same rows already in timeline), flatten tickets_opened/tickets_assigned in get_user_context from search envelopes to plain row lists — so payloads shrink and shapes match what an AI actually needs feat: hardcode search_tickets to creation-date ASC and add emoji status guidance in MCP instructions so ticket lists read oldest-first like a technician's inbox and each status carries a visual cue chore: slim glpi_list_reference categories (drop always-redundant level, emit comment only when non-null) dropping payload from ~66KB to ~46KB so 306-row category trees fit under the MCP response cap

message.content: try to test it for @skills/git-push/ then

content.content:
1	---
2	name: git-push
3	description: Safely commit and push changes. Audits .gitignore for sensitive files, stages with git add ., writes a conventional commit (what + why), creates a GitHub repo via gh if none exists, and pushes. Use when the user says "push", "ship it", "commit and push", or invokes /git-push.
4	---
5	
6	# git-push
7	
8	Safely commit and push the current changes. Every run follows this exact sequence:
9	
10	## 1. Safety check
11	
12	Run `git status` to see what will be staged. Review the actual file list for:
13	
14	- **Secrets** — `.env`, `.pem`, `.key`, credentials, tokens, API keys
15	- **Internal data** — data dumps, migration exports, board exports, JSON/CSV with names/tasks/internal info, database exports. Any folder that looks like a one-off dump (e.g. `*-migration/`, `*-export/`, `*-dump/`) is a red flag.
16	- **Large binaries** — blobs, images, compiled files that don't belong in git
17	- **Files that should be gitignored** — check if the repo is public, and whether the file contains anything that shouldn't be on GitHub
18	- **Local filesystem paths** — grep staged text files (README, docs, configs, examples) for absolute local paths: `C:\Users\...`, `C:/Francisco/...`, `/Users/<name>/...`, `/home/<name>/...`, `/mnt/c/...`, `~/<name>/...`. These leak the author's machine layout and username. Also watch for hardcoded local hostnames, LAN IPs (`192.168.*`, `10.*`), and `localhost:<port>` references that only make sense on the author's box.
19	
20	Local paths are a **fixable cosmetic leak**, not a blocker. When you find one:
21	
22	1. Replace it with a generic placeholder (`/path/to/repo`, `<repo-root>`, `~/projects/<repo>`) or a repo-relative path.
23	2. If the path is inside an example command, keep the command runnable — use a clearly-placeholder token like `<your-username>` so readers know to substitute.
24	3. Re-stage the edited file before committing. Do NOT ask the user for permission to scrub a local path — just fix it and mention it in the heads-up.
25	
26	Only stop if you find a **real problem** (secrets, dumps, binaries). Do NOT theorize about what .gitignore *could* be missing — check the actual files. If every file in the diff is safe, move on silently.
27	
28	## 2. Initialize git if missing
29	
30	If `.git/` does not exist, run `git init` and continue. If it exists, skip.
31	
32	## 3. Stage everything
33	
34	```bash
35	git add .
36	```
37	
38	## 4. Write the commit message
39	
40	Use **conventional commits**. No co-authoring.
41	
42	Every message MUST answer both **what** changed and **why** it changed.
43	
44	- `feat:` for new functionality
45	- `fix:` for bug fixes
46	- `chore:` for everything else
47	
48	Bad: `feat: add repo mapping` — missing the why.
49	Good: `feat: add repo mapping to centralize project navigation`
50	
51	Bad: `fix: update query logic` — what query? why?
52	Good: `fix: deduplicate subsidiary query to avoid double-counting schedules`
53	
54	Bad: `chore: update dependencies` — why now?
55	Good: `chore: bump django to 4.2.9 to patch CVE-2024-XXXXX`
56	
57	Before writing the message, inspect both:
58	
59	- the staged diff
60	- the current chat context / recent user requests
61	
62	Check whether the staged work represents **one logical change** or **multiple logical changes bundled together** because everything was staged with `git add .`.
63	
64	If the diff includes multiple distinct tasks or features, the commit message must say so explicitly instead of pretending everything is one small change. In that case:
65	
66	- write a multi-line conventional commit message
67	- use one `type:` line per logical change
68	- make each line include both **what** changed and **why**
69	- order the lines by importance, with the main user-facing change first
70	
71	Bad multi-change message:
72	`feat: update spotify playback`
73	
74	Good multi-change message:
75	`feat: return playlist context in spotify playback responses to show what started playing
76	feat: add Droid MCP and skill deployment guidance to document the new local setup`
77	
78	Another good multi-change message:
79	`feat: deploy personal skills to Droid to keep Claude and Droid installs in sync
80	docs: update skill guidelines to point at deploy_skills.py and Droid skill folders`
81	
82	Do NOT invent a fake single-feature message when the diff clearly contains unrelated or semi-related work. The message should reflect the true scope of the commit.
83	
84	Read the diff to understand the changes, then write the message. Use a HEREDOC:
85	
86	```bash
87	git commit -m "$(cat <<'EOF'
88	type: what changed and why
89	EOF
90	)"
91	```
92	
93	If there is nothing to commit yet (empty repo), create an initial commit after staging so step 5's `gh repo create --push` has something to push.
94	
95	## 5. Ensure a GitHub remote exists
96	
97	Run `git remote -v`. Three cases:
98	
99	**a) A remote is already set** → skip to step 6.
100	
101	**b) No remote at all** → create one on GitHub using `gh`:
102	
103	1. Verify auth: `gh auth status`. If not logged in, stop and ask the user to run `gh auth login`.
104	2. Pick a repo name. Default: the current directory's basename. Confirm with the user before creating.
105	3. Default to **private** unless the user explicitly says public. Creating a public repo without asking is a mistake — code can contain secrets, half-finished work, or internal context the user didn't mean to publish.
106	4. Create and push in one call:
107	
108	   ```bash
109	   gh repo create <name> --source=. --private --push
110	   ```
111	
112	   This creates the GitHub repo, wires up `origin`, and pushes the current branch with upstream tracking. Do not run `git push` again afterward.
113	
114	**c) Remote exists but the current branch has no upstream** → push with `-u`:
115	
116	```bash
117	git push -u origin <current-branch>
118	```
119	
120	## 6. Push
121	
122	If step 5 already pushed (cases b and c), skip this step. Otherwise:
123	
124	```bash
125	git push
126	```
127	
128	**Read the push output.** If GitHub has renamed or transferred the repo, it prints something like:
129	
130	```
131	remote: This repository moved. Please use the new location:
132	remote:   https://github.com/<org>/<new-name>.git
133	```
134	
135	The push still succeeds via redirect, but the local `origin` still points at the old name. When you see this notice, update the URL on the spot:
136	
137	```bash
138	git remote set-url origin https://github.com/<org>/<new-name>.git
139	```
140	
141	Use the exact URL printed after "new location:". Tell the user you updated the origin URL and show the new value.
142	
143	## Rules
144	
145	- NEVER skip any step.
146	- NEVER push if the audit flagged something — resolve it first.
147	- NEVER add a Co-Authored-By line.
148	- NEVER create a public repo without explicit user consent. Default to private.
149	- NEVER guess the repo name — confirm with the user before `gh repo create`.
150	- If there are no changes to commit and the remote is already up to date, say so and stop.
151	- If a pre-commit hook fails, fix the issue and create a NEW commit (don't amend).
152

file.content:
---
name: git-push
description: Safely commit and push changes. Audits .gitignore for sensitive files, stages with git add ., writes a conventional commit (what + why), creates a GitHub repo via gh if none exists, and pushes. Use when the user says "push", "ship it", "commit and push", or invokes /git-push.
---

# git-push

Safely commit and push the current changes. Every run follows this exact sequence:

## 1. Safety check

Run `git status` to see what will be staged. Review the actual file list for:

- **Secrets** — `.env`, `.pem`, `.key`, credentials, tokens, API keys
- **Internal data** — data dumps, migration exports, board exports, JSON/CSV with names/tasks/internal info, database exports. Any folder that looks like a one-off dump (e.g. `*-migration/`, `*-export/`, `*-dump/`) is a red flag.
- **Large binaries** — blobs, images, compiled files that don't belong in git
- **Files that should be gitignored** — check if the repo is public, and whether the file contains anything that shouldn't be on GitHub
- **Local filesystem paths** — grep staged text files (README, docs, configs, examples) for absolute local paths: `C:\Users\...`, `C:/Francisco/...`, `/Users/<name>/...`, `/home/<name>/...`, `/mnt/c/...`, `~/<name>/...`. These leak the author's machine layout and username. Also watch for hardcoded local hostnames, LAN IPs (`192.168.*`, `10.*`), and `localhost:<port>` references that only make sense on the author's box.

Local paths are a **fixable cosmetic leak**, not a blocker. When you find one:

1. Replace it with a generic placeholder (`/path/to/repo`, `<repo-root>`, `~/projects/<repo>`) or a repo-relative path.
2. If the path is inside an example command, keep the command runnable — use a clearly-placeholder token like `<your-username>` so readers know to substitute.
3. Re-stage the edited file before committing. Do NOT ask the user for permission to scrub a local path — just fix it and mention it in the heads-up.

Only stop if you find a **real problem** (secrets, dumps, binaries). Do NOT theorize about what .gitignore *could* be missing — check the actual files. If every file in the diff is safe, move on silently.

## 2. Initialize git if missing

If `.git/` does not exist, run `git init` and continue. If it exists, skip.

## 3. Stage everything

```bash
git add .
```

## 4. Write the commit message

Use **conventional commits**. No co-authoring.

Every message MUST answer both **what** changed and **why** it changed.

- `feat:` for new functionality
- `fix:` for bug fixes
- `chore:` for everything else

Bad: `feat: add repo mapping` — missing the why.
Good: `feat: add repo mapping to centralize project navigation`

Bad: `fix: update query logic` — what query? why?
Good: `fix: deduplicate subsidiary query to avoid double-counting schedules`

Bad: `chore: update dependencies` — why now?
Good: `chore: bump django to 4.2.9 to patch CVE-2024-XXXXX`

Before writing the message, inspect both:

- the staged diff
- the current chat context / recent user requests

Check whether the staged work represents **one logical change** or **multiple logical changes bundled together** because everything was staged with `git add .`.

If the diff includes multiple distinct tasks or features, the commit message must say so explicitly instead of pretending everything is one small change. In that case:

- write a multi-line conventional commit message
- use one `type:` line per logical change
- make each line include both **what** changed and **why**
- order the lines by importance, with the main user-facing change first

Bad multi-change message:
`feat: update spotify playback`

Good multi-change message:
`feat: return playlist context in spotify playback responses to show what started playing
feat: add Droid MCP and skill deployment guidance to document the new local setup`

Another good multi-change message:
`feat: deploy personal skills to Droid to keep Claude and Droid installs in sync
docs: update skill guidelines to point at deploy_skills.py and Droid skill folders`

Do NOT invent a fake single-feature message when the diff clearly contains unrelated or semi-related work. The message should reflect the true scope of the commit.

Read the diff to understand the changes, then write the message. Use a HEREDOC:

```bash
git commit -m "$(cat <<'EOF'
type: what changed and why
EOF
)"
```

If there is nothing to commit yet (empty repo), create an initial commit after staging so step 5's `gh repo create --push` has something to push.

## 5. Ensure a GitHub remote exists

Run `git remote -v`. Three cases:

**a) A remote is already set** → skip to step 6.

**b) No remote at all** → create one on GitHub using `gh`:

1. Verify auth: `gh auth status`. If not logged in, stop and ask the user to run `gh auth login`.
2. Pick a repo name. Default: the current directory's basename. Confirm with the user before creating.
3. Default to **private** unless the user explicitly says public. Creating a public repo without asking is a mistake — code can contain secrets, half-finished work, or internal context the user didn't mean to publish.
4. Create and push in one call:

   ```bash
   gh repo create <name> --source=. --private --push
   ```

   This creates the GitHub repo, wires up `origin`, and pushes the current branch with upstream tracking. Do not run `git push` again afterward.

**c) Remote exists but the current branch has no upstream** → push with `-u`:

```bash
git push -u origin <current-branch>
```

## 6. Push

If step 5 already pushed (cases b and c), skip this step. Otherwise:

```bash
git push
```

**Read the push output.** If GitHub has renamed or transferred the repo, it prints something like:

```
remote: This repository moved. Please use the new location:
remote:   https://github.com/<org>/<new-name>.git
```

The push still succeeds via redirect, but the local `origin` still points at the old name. When you see this notice, update the URL on the spot:

```bash
git remote set-url origin https://github.com/<org>/<new-name>.git
```

Use the exact URL printed after "new location:". Tell the user you updated the origin URL and show the new value.

## Rules

- NEVER skip any step.
- NEVER push if the audit flagged something — resolve it first.
- NEVER add a Co-Authored-By line.
- NEVER create a public repo without explicit user consent. Default to private.
- NEVER guess the repo name — confirm with the user before `gh repo create`.
- If there are no changes to commit and the remote is already up to date, say so and stop.

content.text:
Base directory for this skill: /home/claude/.claude/skills/git-push

# git-push

Safely commit and push the current changes. Every run follows this exact sequence:

## 1. Safety check

Run `git status` to see what will be staged. Review the actual file list for:

- **Secrets** — `.env`, `.pem`, `.key`, credentials, tokens, API keys
- **Internal data** — data dumps, migration exports, board exports, JSON/CSV with names/tasks/internal info, database exports. Any folder that looks like a one-off dump (e.g. `*-migration/`, `*-export/`, `*-dump/`) is a red flag.
- **Large binaries** — blobs, images, compiled files that don't belong in git
- **Files that should be gitignored** — check if the repo is public, and whether the file contains anything that shouldn't be on GitHub
- **Local filesystem paths** — grep staged text files (README, docs, configs, examples) for absolute local paths: `C:\Users\...`, `C:/Francisco/...`, `/Users/<name>/...`, `/home/<name>/...`, `/mnt/c/...`, `~/<name>/...`. These leak the author's machine layout and username. Also watch for hardcoded local hostnames, LAN IPs (`192.168.*`, `10.*`), and `localhost:<port>` references that only make sense on the author's box.

Local paths are a **fixable cosmetic leak**, not a blocker. When you find one:

1. Replace it with a generic placeholder (`/path/to/repo`, `<repo-root>`, `~/projects/<repo>`) or a repo-relative path.
2. If the path is inside an example command, keep the command runnable — use a clearly-placeholder token like `<your-username>` so readers know to substitute.
3. Re-stage the edited file before committing. Do NOT ask the user for permission to scrub a local path — just fix it and mention it in the heads-up.

Only stop if you find a **real problem** (secrets, dumps, binaries). Do NOT theorize about what .gitignore *could* be missing — check the actual files. If every file in the diff is safe, move on silently.

## 2. Initialize git if missing

If `.git/` does not exist, run `git init` and continue. If it exists, skip.

## 3. Stage everything

```bash
git add .
```

## 4. Write the commit message

Use **conventional commits**. No co-authoring.

Every message MUST answer both **what** changed and **why** it changed.

- `feat:` for new functionality
- `fix:` for bug fixes
- `chore:` for everything else

Bad: `feat: add repo mapping` — missing the why.
Good: `feat: add repo mapping to centralize project navigation`

Bad: `fix: update query logic` — what query? why?
Good: `fix: deduplicate subsidiary query to avoid double-counting schedules`

Bad: `chore: update dependencies` — why now?
Good: `chore: bump django to 4.2.9 to patch CVE-2024-XXXXX`

Before writing the message, inspect both:

- the staged diff
- the current chat context / recent user requests

Check whether the staged work represents **one logical change** or **multiple logical changes bundled together** because everything was staged with `git add .`.

If the diff includes multiple distinct tasks or features, the commit message must say so explicitly instead of pretending everything is one small change. In that case:

- write a multi-line conventional commit message
- use one `type:` line per logical change
- make each line include both **what** changed and **why**
- order the lines by importance, with the main user-facing change first

Bad multi-change message:
`feat: update spotify playback`

Good multi-change message:
`feat: return playlist context in spotify playback responses to show what started playing
feat: add Droid MCP and skill deployment guidance to document the new local setup`

Another good multi-change message:
`feat: deploy personal skills to Droid to keep Claude and Droid installs in sync
docs: update skill guidelines to point at deploy_skills.py and Droid skill folders`

Do NOT invent a fake single-feature message when the diff clearly contains unrelated or semi-related work. The message should reflect the true scope of the commit.

Read the diff to understand the changes, then write the message. Use a HEREDOC:

```bash
git commit -m "$(cat <<'EOF'
type: what changed and why
EOF
)"
```

If there is nothing to commit yet (empty repo), create an initial commit after staging so step 5's `gh repo create --push` has something to push.

## 5. Ensure a GitHub remote exists

Run `git remote -v`. Three cases:

**a) A remote is already set** → skip to step 6.

**b) No remote at all** → create one on GitHub using `gh`:

1. Verify auth: `gh auth status`. If not logged in, stop and ask the user to run `gh auth login`.
2. Pick a repo name. Default: the current directory's basename. Confirm with the user before creating.
3. Default to **private** unless the user explicitly says public. Creating a public repo without asking is a mistake — code can contain secrets, half-finished work, or internal context the user didn't mean to publish.
4. Create and push in one call:

   ```bash
   gh repo create <name> --source=. --private --push
   ```

   This creates the GitHub repo, wires up `origin`, and pushes the current branch with upstream tracking. Do not run `git push` again afterward.

**c) Remote exists but the current branch has no upstream** → push with `-u`:

```bash
git push -u origin <current-branch>
```

## 6. Push

If step 5 already pushed (cases b and c), skip this step. Otherwise:

```bash
git push
```

**Read the push output.** If GitHub has renamed or transferred the repo, it prints something like:

```
remote: This repository moved. Please use the new location:
remote:   https://github.com/<org>/<new-name>.git
```

The push still succeeds via redirect, but the local `origin` still points at the old name. When you see this notice, update the URL on the spot:

```bash
git remote set-url origin https://github.com/<org>/<new-name>.git
```

Use the exact URL printed after "new location:". Tell the user you updated the origin URL and show the new value.

## Rules

- NEVER skip any step.
- NEVER push if the audit flagged something — resolve it first.
- NEVER add a Co-Authored-By line.
- NEVER create a public repo without explicit user consent. Default to private.
- NEVER guess the repo name — confirm with the user before `gh repo create`.
- If there are no changes to commit and the remote is already up to date, say so and stop.
- If a pre-commit hook fails, fix the issue and create a NEW commit (don't amend).

**Steps 4–6 — Commit and push**

input.command:
git commit -m "$(cat <<'EOF'
fix: strip ANSI color codes in deploy_skills.py when stdout isn't a capable terminal so output is readable on Windows cmd.exe and when redirected to a file — adds _supports_color() that returns False for non-ttys and calls os.system("") on win32 to flip virtual terminal processing on (Windows 10+), then renders RED/GREEN/YELLOW/RESET as empty strings when unsupported instead of leaking raw escape sequences like [32m into the output
chore: gitignore .entire/ so the entire checkpoint/search tool's local logs, metadata, and tmp state stop showing up as untracked files on every git status
EOF
)"
input.description: Commit cross-platform color fix and gitignore update
